ideas.Four roots: earth, water, air, fire:
The doctrine of the "**four roots**" (*rhizōmata*) — **earth, water, air, and fire** — is Empedocles's most famous and influential contribution: the origin of the theory of the **four classical elements** that would dominate Western and Mediterranean science and medicine for over two thousand years, until the chemical revolution of the modern era. Empedocles proposed that these four eternal, unchanging substances are the fundamental building blocks of everything in the cosmos.

Empedocles developed this theory partly in response to the great challenge of **Parmenides**, who had argued that genuine coming-to-be and passing-away are impossible, since nothing can arise from what-is-not. Empedocles ingeniously accepted Parmenides's principle while saving the reality of the changing world. The four roots themselves, he agreed, are ungenerated and imperishable — they never truly come into being or perish, but exist eternally and unchangeably, as Being must. What we *call* birth, death, and change is really nothing but the **mixing and separating** of these four permanent elements in different proportions. A bone, a tree, a person — each is a particular blend of earth, water, air, and fire combined in a specific ratio.

This was a brilliant and durable solution. It preserved a plurality of basic realities (against Parmenidean monism) while honoring the demand that the fundamental stuff be eternal and unchanging. And it explained the qualitative diversity of nature as arising from different *combinations* of a few basic ingredients — a genuinely fruitful scientific idea. The four elements, taken up by Aristotle (who added a fifth, aether, for the heavens) and by the physician Galen (who linked them to the four humors), became the foundation of physics, chemistry, cosmology, and medicine for millennia.
ideas.Love and Strife as cosmic forces:
Alongside his four material **roots** (earth, water, air, fire), Empedocles posited two great **motive forces** that act upon them: **Love** (*Philia*) and **Strife** (*Neikos*). These are the cosmic powers that drive all change by mixing and separating the elements — an ingenious addition that supplies what a mere theory of inert material elements lacks: an explanation of *why* and *how* the elements combine and dissolve, of the source of motion and change in the universe.

**Love** is the force of **attraction, union, and combination** — the principle that draws the disparate elements *together*, mixing and uniting them to form compound things, harmony, and living beings. **Strife** is the opposing force of **repulsion, division, and separation** — the principle that drives the elements *apart*, dissolving compounds and separating like from unlike. All the coming-to-be and passing-away in the world, all mixture and dissolution, results from the perpetual contest between these two forces working on the four eternal roots.

Empedocles treated Love and Strife not as abstract mechanical forces but in richly quasi-**mythical**, even divine terms, and their conflict has a moral and religious resonance in his poetry (Love being associated with harmony and the good, Strife with conflict). This introduction of *distinct causes of motion*, separate from the material elements themselves, was philosophically significant — Aristotle noted it as an early groping toward the recognition of an "efficient cause" and a source of goodness in the world. By pairing four passive material principles with two active moving forces, Empedocles constructed one of the most complete and influential physical systems of the Presocratic era.
ideas.Cycles of mixture and separation:
Empedocles wove his four **roots** and the two forces of **Love** and **Strife** into a grand vision of the universe as an eternal, repeating **cosmic cycle** — a rhythmic alternation between unity and multiplicity, mixture and separation, driven by the waxing and waning dominance of the two opposing forces. This cyclical cosmology is one of the most striking and comprehensive world-pictures of early Greek philosophy.

The cycle has, on the standard interpretation, four phases governed by the shifting balance of power between Love and Strife. At one extreme stands the reign of total **Love**: here all four elements are perfectly and completely blended together into a homogeneous, harmonious unity — a divine, spherical, blessed whole that Empedocles calls the "**Sphere**" (*Sphairos*), in which no distinct things exist, only perfect mixture. Then **Strife** begins to enter and grow, progressively separating the elements, breaking up the unity, and generating the world of distinct, individual things — including our present cosmos, with its living creatures. As Strife increases toward total dominance, the elements are wholly separated out, each gathered apart into its own kind — complete disunity.

Then the movement reverses: **Love** grows again, drawing the separated elements back together, generating a world once more (now on the way *toward* unity), until perfect mixture is restored and the Sphere reconstituted — whereupon the whole cycle begins again, endlessly. Living things, including us, arise during the intermediate phases when the elements are partly mixed and partly separated. This vision of an eternally recurring cosmic rhythm — unity dissolving into multiplicity and multiplicity returning to unity — is a profound and imaginative attempt to comprehend the whole history of the cosmos as a single, law-governed, repeating process.

works.On Nature:
*On Nature* (*Peri Physeōs*) is the philosophical poem in which Empedocles set out his physical and cosmological system — his account of the ultimate constituents of reality and the forces that govern them. Like several Presocratic works, it survives only in **fragments** (though Empedocles is one of the better-preserved of the early thinkers, with several hundred lines extant), quoted by later authors, especially Aristotle, Simplicius, and others. Notably, Empedocles chose to write not in prose (as the Milesians had) but in **epic verse** (dactylic hexameter, the meter of Homer), giving his natural philosophy a grand, incantatory, poetic power.

The poem presents the core doctrines of Empedocles's physics. It expounds the theory of the **four roots** (earth, water, air, and fire) as the eternal, unchangeable elements of which everything is composed, and the two cosmic forces of **Love** and **Strife**, whose perpetual contest mixes and separates the roots to produce all the coming-to-be and passing-away in the world. It develops his grand **cyclical cosmology** — the eternal alternation between the unity of the "Sphere" under Love and the dispersion of the elements under Strife. And it addresses a wide range of natural phenomena: the formation of the cosmos, the nature of the heavenly bodies, and — famously — the origin and physiology of **living things**, including his remarkable proto-evolutionary account of how animal forms arose from the random combination of body parts, with only the viable combinations surviving, and his pioneering theory of **perception** (like being known by like). Addressed to his pupil Pausanias, *On Nature* is a foundational document of Greek natural philosophy and one of the great achievements of Presocratic thought.
works.Purifications (fragments):
The *Purifications* (*Katharmoi*) is the second of the two poems traditionally attributed to Empedocles — a religious, mystical, and moral work, in striking contrast (or perhaps complement) to the natural-philosophical *On Nature*. It too survives only in **fragments**, and it reveals a very different side of this extraordinary figure, who was at once a natural philosopher, a religious teacher and prophet, a healer, and (by his own account) a kind of divine being. Indeed, scholars have long debated the relationship between the two poems, and even whether they are separate works or parts of one — but the *Purifications* fragments are unmistakably religious and eschatological in character.

The *Purifications* sets out a doctrine of the **soul**, sin, and salvation reminiscent of **Pythagorean** and Orphic religion, centered on the **transmigration of souls** (reincarnation). Empedocles teaches that souls are fallen, exiled divinities (*daimones*), condemned for some primal transgression — an act of bloodshed or the breaking of an oath, associated with the reign of **Strife** — to a long cycle of rebirths, passing through many bodily forms (plant, animal, human) over vast stretches of time as a punishment and purification. In one famous fragment Empedocles declares: "For I have already been once a boy and a girl, a bush and a bird and a mute fish in the sea."

The poem prescribes the path of **purification** by which the soul may cleanse itself and eventually escape the cycle to be restored to divinity — including strict **abstinence from bloodshed and meat-eating** (since killing an animal might be killing a reincarnated kinsman). Empedocles famously opens by proclaiming himself "an immortal god, no longer mortal." Combining religion, ethics, and cosmic myth, the *Purifications* reveals the profoundly spiritual dimension of Empedocles's thought.
